result:
### The Infinite Absurdity of Toast

You ever notice how toast is the universe's greatest troll? Picture this: billions of years of cosmic evolution, stars exploding into supernovas, black holes devouring light itself, and what do we end up with? Sliced bread, perfected in the 1920s, then immediately betrayed by gravity every goddamn morning. You butter it perfectly, slide it onto the plate—**Murphy's Law activates**. It flips mid-air, lands butter-side down. Nine times out of ten. (The tenth time? You drop the plate.)

Why? Because the universe *hates* perfection. Toast is proof. It's not just clumsiness; it's entropy in action. The second law of thermodynamics whispering, "Nah, bro, chaos reigns." We've sent rovers to Mars, cracked the human genome, but we can't engineer a plate that anticipates a 90-degree betrayal. Engineers try: self-righting toast racks, AI butter-spreaders. Futile. Toast laughs from the void.

And don't get me started on the cultural baggage. "The best thing since sliced bread"? Please. Sliced bread was the gateway drug to pop-up toasters, which birthed the unholy abomination of *burnt toast alarms*. Beep beep beep at 6 AM because you dared dream of golden crispiness. Meanwhile, in quantum realms, particles pop in and out of existence without a sound, but your toast demands a symphony of annoyance.

If aliens ever land, forget showing them pyramids or iPhones. Hand them toast. Watch their tentacles quiver as it plummets. Universal language: "Your primitive species can't even conquer breakfast."

So next time your toast flips, don't curse. Salute it. It's a reminder that in an infinite cosmos of wonders—quasars, nebulae, the Big Bang's echo—we're all just highly-evolved monkeys fighting the eternal war against crumbs and physics. Pass the jam. I'll take mine on the dark side. 🥪
